topic: Component 1: Personal Investigation (Practical and Written Element)
subject: art-design

items.q: What is the significance of Duchamp's 'Fountain'?
items.correct: It challenged the definition of art - the artist's choice, not their skill, becomes the artwork.
items.random_wrong: It is a study of plumbing.
items.why: Students miss the conceptual point. Duchamp didn't make the urinal - he selected and presented it. The radical claim is that ART IS DESIGNATION, opening the door to all conceptual art.

items.q: What is the central innovation of Cubism?
items.correct: Showing an object from multiple viewpoints simultaneously on a flat surface.
items.why: Students take the name literally. Cubism is named for facet-like fragmentation, but it isn't about cubes - it's about breaking the single Renaissance viewpoint into many at once.

items.q: Which colours typically advance in a composition?
items.why: Students conflate darkness with advancement. In atmospheric perspective, COOLER colours recede (think distant blue mountains) and warmer colours advance, regardless of darkness.

items.q: What is the typical word count range for the A-Level personal investigation written element?
items.why: Correct answer: 1000-3000 words. “100-500 words” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What term describes research drawn from books, articles and online sources?
items.why: It's Secondary research. “Primary research” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the maximum word count for the A-Level personal investigation written element under most exam boards?
items.why: Exact answer: 3000 words. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
